paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: I've been dying to ask you about the retreat that you put on. Before I begin, what I want to talk about is how important it is for me that this happened, because it's the first time that AOA has a retreat that is not my brainchild and Tara's brainchild. It's actually the community growing, and now we have, for the first time, someone else putting out a retreat.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: I put on a retreat called Welcoming Fear. The idea was to take the kind of work we've done in AOA and bring it out into nature, into the wilderness, and into very present physically embodied experiences with fear. It's an invitation for people to really sensuously sit with and enjoy and just notice and learn about the way that their body and their emotional system and their mind process fear. It wasn't goal-oriented, like you're going to come and jump off a cliff. It was something far more slow and deliberate.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: We camped under the stars in the desert near Moab, Utah. There were two rigged activities led by world-class guides. One was a highline—walking on a one-inch wide piece of nylon webbing strung between two cliffs, hundreds of feet in the air. We had it rigged with another line above so a beginner could do the walk. This was a slow way of approaching your fear, step after step, feeling how your body responds and where your attention goes. Then we had the faster way of working with fear: connecting to a rope and jumping off a cliff, free falling for several seconds before the rope catches you and swings you away from the cliff.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: What was your hope for the outcome for the people you were serving in the retreat?

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: My hope for the outcome was not for people to come through having learned an ability to conquer their fear. What I really wanted was for people to have developed an enjoyment of the process of being with their fear and not needing to do anything with it, not having something that needs to happen, not pushing through it, not running from it, but just noticing what happens when they approach their fears in a very subtle step-by-step way.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: For that, I need to scroll back to some of my history. When I got into adventure sports, I had adopted the narrative, which is a common societal narrative, that fear was some kind of oppressor for me, that it was something I needed to overcome or conquer. Over decades of doing that, I noticed that the people who stayed in that mode—conquering their fears, pushing them away, skipping over them—ended up having the most accidents, injuries, deaths, and also grew at the slowest rate.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: The people who had been in the sport the longest with the cleanest safety records, the real mentors, were the ones who really developed a relationship with their fear where they just wore it on their sleeve. They would show up to an exit point and just name all the things they were scared of and be visibly feeling it, but not in a dysregulated way. It would just be like, here it is. Here's the things I'm concerned about. Or, oh yeah, I've got the fear poops. I've got to poop because I'm scared, just kind of playing with it. Over time, I noticed there was a very big difference in both the enjoyment and the safety record of people who were really developing a loving, sensuous relationship with their fear.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: We spent a day before we did any of the activities just doing introspective work. We had people crawl up to the edge of a cliff and peer their head over, then just meditate and ponder on a series of invitations. That started to dredge up some of their fears, some of their identity fears. There were really two main categories: the fear of death, including their identity, and social fears—what happens to my partner or my business if I go. By the time we got to the edge of a cliff to either walk the highline or to jump, everyone is feeling some level of fear, and it's completely understandable. Here it is, fear of annihilation.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: People got to see the different ways fear moves through individuals and consolidates into roles in a group. There was the person that held the role of anger. There was a person who held the hot potato of being the helper and supporter. I'm the one that's gonna be silent, hanging out in the back, watching everyone else.
